title_en: Qiyas in the Mustasfa: Detailed Analysis
content_en:
Al-Ghazali's treatment of qiyas — analogical reasoning — in the Mustasfa is the most technically elaborate section of the work and represents one of the most rigorous analyses of legal analogy in the classical tradition. He approaches qiyas not as a topic to be described but as a logical operation to be analyzed: what are its components, what conditions must each component satisfy, how is the operative ratio legis (illah) identified and verified, and in what circumstances may an analogy be overridden?

The structure of a valid qiyas requires four elements, and al-Ghazali examines each in turn. The asl (original case) is the case for which a ruling already exists in the Quran, Sunnah, or established ijma. The far' (new case) is the case for which a ruling is sought and not found directly in the sources. The hukm (ruling) is the legal determination of the original case that is to be extended to the new case. The illah (effective cause or ratio legis) is the quality or attribute shared by both the original and new cases that justifies the extension of the ruling from one to the other.

The identification of the illah is the most demanding part of qiyas, and al-Ghazali devotes most of his analysis to it. The illah must be an attribute that is relevant to the ruling — meaning it must make sense that the Sharia attached the ruling to this attribute rather than to some other feature of the original case. It must be found in the new case — meaning the analogy only works if the far' actually shares the relevant attribute with the asl. It must be a consistent attribute — meaning the rule should apply wherever the attribute is found, not selectively. And it must not be overridden by a contrary text in the specific case of the far'.

Al-Ghazali classifies the methods for identifying the illah into several types. The clearest case is when the Quran or Sunnah explicitly names the operative cause — this eliminates uncertainty about the ratio and makes the analogy almost as certain as the original ruling. More commonly, the illah must be inferred through one of several recognized procedures: noticing that the ruling varies with a particular attribute (suggesting that attribute is causally relevant), identifying the attribute that the legal experts universally link to the ruling, or selecting among candidate attributes using the principle of appropriate fit (munasaba) — the attribute that most plausibly explains why the Sharia would attach the ruling is most likely the operative cause.

Al-Ghazali also addresses objections (i'tirad) to analogies. A proposed qiyas may be challenged by showing that the illah is absent from the new case, that the illah is present but a contrary text applies to the new case specifically, that the original ruling is exceptional and should not be extended, or that the proposed illah leads to results that are clearly contrary to the Sharia when applied consistently. This adversarial dimension of qiyas — the logic of objection and rebuttal — was a live feature of juridical debate in al-Ghazali's era, conducted in formal disputation sessions, and the Mustasfa's treatment reflects the sophisticated dialectical culture of classical Islamic legal scholarship.

The practical significance of al-Ghazali's treatment of qiyas cannot be overstated. Analogical reasoning is the primary tool by which Islamic law addresses novel situations not directly covered by the Quran or Sunnah, and the rigor with which the Mustasfa analyzes its conditions and limits helped establish the standards that subsequent generations of usul scholars applied to this most consequential form of legal reasoning.
